title: Weekly Hypertrophy Volume Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Weekly Training Volume for Muscle Hypertrophy
description: Calculate optimal weekly sets per muscle group for hypertrophy using Schoenfeld's evidence-based guidelines.
intro: Training volume—the total number of effective sets performed each week—is one of the primary drivers of muscle growth. This calculator uses evidence-based guidelines from Dr. Brad Schoenfeld's research to determine optimal weekly set ranges for each muscle group based on your training level. Whether you're new to strength training or an advanced lifter, proper volume management is crucial for consistent hypertrophy while avoiding overtraining. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: Based on **Schoenfeld's hypertrophy guidelines**, optimal weekly training volume ranges from 10–22 total sets per muscle group depending on experience level. This calculator applies the formula directly to your inputs, providing minimum, optimal, and maximum sustainable volume thresholds.
useCases: Programming strength training splits and workout frequency
Validating your current volume against scientific recommendations
Coaches designing customized training plans for clients
Quick reference for weekly set targets during training
explanation:
## How Hypertrophy Training Volume Works

Training volume—measured in total sets per muscle group per week—is one of the three primary pillars of muscle growth, alongside mechanical tension and metabolic stress. Dr. Brad Schoenfeld's meta-analyses established evidence-based guidelines for optimal volume across different experience levels.

## How This Calculator Works

This calculator applies Schoenfeld's hypertrophy volume framework to your inputs. Based on your training level and target muscle group, it displays:

- **Minimum sets**: The bare-minimum effective volume for muscle growth
- **Optimal sets**: The sweet spot for maximizing hypertrophy with good recovery
- **Maximum tolerable**: The upper threshold before diminishing returns and excessive fatigue

## Weekly Set Recommendations by Training Level

| Training Level | Sets Per Muscle Group |
|---|---|
| Beginner | 10–12 sets/week |
| Intermediate | 12–18 sets/week |
| Advanced | 16–22 sets/week |

These guidelines assume sets taken close to muscular failure (RPE 7–9 on a 1–10 scale).

## Key Principles for Success

- **Volume distribution**: Spread your weekly sets across 2–3 training sessions per muscle group for optimal recovery and rep quality
- **Exercise selection**: Build your program around compound movements (squats, bench press, deadlifts, rows) before adding isolation work
- **Progressive overload**: Increase volume gradually over weeks and months, not days
- **Individual variation**: Genetics, recovery capacity, sleep, and nutrition affect your Max Recoverable Volume (MRV)
- **Deload weeks**: Every 4–8 weeks, reduce volume by ~40% to allow nervous system and tissue recovery
- **Consistency**: Adherence matters more than perfect volume—steady work beats sporadic high-volume sessions

This calculator is for educational guidance. For a personalized program, consult a certified strength coach or sports scientist.

faq.q: What is training volume and why does it matter for muscle growth?
faq.a: Training volume (total sets per muscle per week) is one of the primary drivers of hypertrophy. Higher volume creates greater muscle protein synthesis stimulus, leading to bigger gains in size and strength.

faq.q: How many sets per week do I need as a beginner?
faq.a: Beginners typically benefit from 10–12 sets per muscle group per week. This provides sufficient stimulus for growth while allowing proper recovery and technique refinement.

faq.q: What's the optimal weekly volume for intermediate lifters?
faq.a: Intermediate lifters (1–3 years of consistent training) should aim for 12–18 sets per muscle group weekly, ideally spread across 2–3 sessions for better recovery.

faq.q: How much volume can advanced lifters handle?
faq.a: Advanced lifters (3+ years of serious training) typically tolerate 16–22 sets per muscle group per week. Your personal Max Recoverable Volume (MRV) depends on genetics, sleep, and nutrition.

faq.q: Should I do all my sets in one session or split them across the week?
faq.a: Always split your weekly volume across 2–3 sessions per muscle. This improves recovery, maintains rep quality, and typically leads to better long-term gains than high-volume single sessions.

faq.q: What happens if I exceed maximum tolerable volume?
faq.a: Excessive volume leads to form breakdown, plateaus, excessive fatigue, and injury risk. Start conservative, monitor your recovery, and increase gradually—more isn't always better.

faq.q: How often should I take a deload week?
faq.a: Most lifters benefit from a deload (40% volume reduction) every 4–8 weeks. This allows muscle and nervous system recovery, preventing overuse injuries and mental burnout.

faq.q: Do smaller muscle groups like biceps need different volume?
faq.a: Smaller muscles may tolerate slightly different volumes, but these guidelines are a solid starting point. Track your progress and adjust based on recovery and results.

faq.q: Does volume matter more than exercise selection?
faq.a: Both matter. The best program combines appropriate volume with quality compound exercises (squats, deadlifts, rows, presses) and consistent progressive overload.
